conversation.statement: Sure, but I'm afraid there's not much I can tell you.
I don't know... all of a sudden there were flames, right in front of the space heater...
I was with Trevor in his living room watching TV.
I wish I knew! I screamed at him, yelling he should get up, but then there was all this smoke, and I panicked and ran out...
Prescription what? No, I didn't know that.
conversation.response: May I ask you some questions about what happened last night?
Where were you at the time the fire started?
Why was Mr. Johnson unable to escape the fire?
We found gasoline in the flooring around the space heater. How do you explain that?
Were you aware that your boyfriend abused prescription oxycodone?
So you haven't seen any pills in his house?

conversation.name: End
conversation.statement: Why are you here? I already told the officer everything.
How... okay, I admit that Trevor and I took oxycodone, but please don't tell my family!

No, I have taken it a couple of times before.
Trevor said it was safe because it's a prescription drug, so I didn't think twice about it.

Ever since I knew him.
He always said the pills made him feel better, less depressed, you know. He was getting really messed up without them.

I don't know how he usually got them, but one time he took my sister's prescription.
I thought it was okay since she didn't need it anymore.

Remember when I told you that I couldn't wake him up?
Well that was because Trevor was dead. DEAD!

When I found Trevor dead, I knew that everything would come out... the drugs, the stealing... So I took the gasoline from the garage, poured it over the space heater, and set it on fire.
I didn’t hurt him - I just couldn’t let the drugs ruin my future!
conversation.response: There was oxycodone in your blood the night of the fire.
Was this the first time you abused oxycodone?
How long had Trevor abused the drug?
How did you get the drugs?
Trevor died from an oxycodone overdose.
I'm not sure if arson looks better on your resume.
